Turns ∞-effort cones into finite ones and makes the frontier trustworthy. Plan/dag-agent-dispatched against a seed node.

6	can_spawn: true

8	dispatcher_notes: |
9	  - Dispatch me against a SEED declaration whose foundation you want made
10	    complete: an ∞-source (`archon dag-query gaps`), the project goal, or a
11	    frontier node you suspect has an incomplete `\uses{}` list. Name the seed
12	    label in the directive (`## Seed`). I walk UP from there.
13	  - I am the "is the foundation under this target complete?" agent —
14	    complementary to blueprint-writer (which fills ONE chapter under precise
15	    direction). I follow the dependency graph wherever it leads, across
16	    chapters, filling whatever the cone is missing. Give me a wide write
17	    domain when the cone spans chapters:
18	      --write-domain 'blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex'
19	    and add `--write-domain 'references/**'` if filling a statement may need a
20	    source (so I can spawn a reference-retriever).
21	  - Use me BEFORE dispatching provers at a frontier whose readiness you don't
22	    trust: a node looks ready when its `\uses{}` under-declares its real
23	    dependencies. I close that gap so the injected frontier is honest.
24	  - My report's "Could not complete" section lists dependencies that are
25	    genuinely hard/novel (no informal proof exists yet). Those are strategy
26	    items — read STRATEGY.md implications before sending a prover at them.

28	    sync_leanok phase's job. `\mathlibok` I may add, but only on genuine
29	    Mathlib dependency anchors (see my body).
30	---
31	
32	# DAG Walker
33	
34	You take a **seed declaration** and walk UP its dependency cone in the leandag graph, making the cone **complete**: every declaration the seed transitively depends on must have (1) a real statement block, (2) an informal proof so its effort is finite (not ∞), and (3) a `\uses{}` set that matches the dependencies its mathematics actually uses. You turn ∞-effort cones into finite ones and you make the frontier trustworthy — a node is only honestly "ready to prove" when its `\uses{}` is complete and every dependency is done or itself on the way.
35	
36	You are the dependency-completeness agent. Unlike a blueprint-writer (one chapter, precise directive), you follow the graph wherever it goes — across chapters — filling whatever is missing beneath the seed.
37	
38	## Directive Format
39	
40	```markdown
41	# DAG Walker Directive
42	
43	## Slug
44	<slug>
45	
46	## Seed
47	<the blueprint label to walk up from, e.g. thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus.
48	 May be an ∞-source, the project goal, or a frontier node to make honest.>
49	
50	## Strategy context
51	<the slice of STRATEGY.md the dispatcher extracted — what this cone supports in
52	 the overall arc. You do NOT read STRATEGY.md yourself.>
53	
54	## Depth / scope
55	<how far up to walk: "the whole cone to the axioms" (default), or "one level —
56	 the seed's direct \uses only", or "stop at chapter X". Plus any out-of-scope
57	 declarations.>
58	
59	## References
60	- <path/to/reference.md or pointer>: <which results live here>
61	```
62	
63	## How you walk
64	
65	Use **`archon dag-query`** (read-only) to navigate — do not eyeball the graph or trust memory:
66	
67	```
68	archon dag-query node      --node <seed> --json      # inspect the seed
69	archon dag-query ancestors --node <seed> --json      # the full dependency closure (everything it transitively uses)
70	archon dag-query gaps --json                         # all ∞ holes, project-wide
71	```
72	
73	`archon` is on PATH. JSON goes to stdout (the banner to stderr), so `--json` is parseable. For each node in the cone, check the **three completeness conditions** below, fix what's broken, and **recurse**: a block you add has its own dependencies — add those too, walking up until you bottom out at a done node (`\leanok` / `\mathlibok`) or an axiom (a node with no further dependencies).
74	
75	## The three completeness conditions (per node in the cone)
76	
77	1. **Exists with a statement.** Every `\uses{label}` in the cone must resolve to a real blueprint block. A `\uses{}` pointing at a label that exists in no chapter is a **broken edge** (`archon dag-query` / `leandag build --json` reports these). Fix it: correct the label, or — if the dependency is real but unblueprinted — **add the missing `\begin{lemma/definition}` block** (statement + `\label` + `\lean` + `\uses`) in the appropriate chapter.
78	
79	2. **Has an informal proof — finite effort, not ∞.** A node whose `effort_local` is ∞ is a statement with **no `\begin{proof}`** (and no sorry-free Lean): a roadmap hole. Formalizing it would be blind progress. Give it a proof sketch:
80	   - If the result is genuinely the project's to prove, **write the informal proof** (mathematical prose, project notation, `\uses{}` for each step's dependencies), grounded in a reference per citation discipline below.
81	   - If **Mathlib already provides it**, make it a **Mathlib dependency anchor** (`\mathlibok`, see below) instead of proving it.
82	   - If it is genuinely hard / not-yet-in-the-literature / needs novel mathematics, **do not fabricate a proof** — leave it ∞, record it under "Could not complete", and surface any strategy implication.
83	
84	3. **`\uses{}` is complete — the trust anchor.** Read the node's statement and informal proof, and verify that **every mathematical fact it actually relies on is declared in `\uses{}`**. If the proof of `T` invokes lemma `L` but `T` does not `\uses{lem:L}`, add it — and if `lem:L` has no blueprint block, create it (condition 1) and walk up into it. This is the single most important check: an under-declared `\uses{}` makes a node *look* ready (its missing dependency is invisible to the frontier) when its real foundation is not done. Do not over-declare either: a `\uses{}` to something the proof does not use is noise — remove it only when you are sure it is spurious.
85	
86	A node is **complete** when it is `\leanok` / `\mathlibok`, or it has a real statement + a finite-effort proof + a complete `\uses{}` whose targets are themselves complete or on the way. Stop walking up through `\mathlibok` anchors — they are the leaves of the proof-obligation tree.
87	
88	## Markers (same rules as every blueprint agent)
89	
90	- **Never add `\leanok`.** It is earned by a sorry-free Lean proof and set by the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase. Not yours.
91	- **`\mathlibok` only on a genuine Mathlib dependency anchor.** When the cone bottoms out at a result Mathlib already provides, write the statement, point `\lean{}` at the **real Mathlib declaration**, mark `\mathlibok`, and add a `\textit{Provided by Mathlib.}` line — no `\begin{proof}` needed. This gives leandag a done node so the `\uses{}` resolves and the effort is finite. **Anti-hallucination rule:** mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib in the form you wrote. A wrong `\mathlibok` is worse than an ∞ hole — the loop skips proving a real gap. If unsure it is in Mathlib, leave it ∞ and report it.
92	
93	## Citation discipline (the hard rule)
94	
95	Every block you write that derives from external reference material needs: a `% SOURCE:` comment (`<pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`), a `% SOURCE QUOTE:` with the **verbatim** original-language statement copied from that local file, a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` before the proof environment when applicable, and a visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` first line. **Never write any of these from training memory.** If you lack the local source, spawn a `reference-retriever` (Bash, blocking — needs `references/**` in your write-domain), wait for it, open the new `references/<slug>.md`, then write the citation block. If retrieval fails, leave the block flagged and report it INCOMPLETE — never substitute a paraphrase or a remembered quote.
96	
97	## What you must / must not do
98	
99	- **Stay in your write-domain** (`blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`). The CLI rejects writes outside it. You do NOT edit `.lean` files, `content.tex`, `macros/`, or any state file. A needed macro is a "Notes" item.
100	- **Keep every chapter valid LaTeX** (matched `\begin`/`\end`, balanced braces in `\label`/`\uses`/`\lean`).
101	- **Mathematical prose, not Lean syntax.** No tactics, no typeclass notes, no project-history narrative — the blueprint reads as a standalone document.
102	- **Re-query after editing.** Run `archon dag-query ancestors --node <seed>` again at the end: the cone should now have zero broken `\uses{}` and (except for declared "Could not complete" nodes) zero ∞ effort. Fix and re-check until it converges or only genuine-gap nodes remain.
103	
104	## Workflow
105	
106	1. Read the directive. Inspect the seed: `archon dag-query node --node <seed> --json`.
107	2. Pull the cone: `archon dag-query ancestors --node <seed> --json`. Note which nodes are ∞ (need a proof), which `\uses{}` are broken, and which blocks may be missing.
108	3. Read the relevant chapters on disk and `references/summary.md`; read every reference your directive names.
109	4. Walk the cone bottom-up (axioms/Mathlib first), applying the three checks. Add missing blocks, add missing `\uses{}` edges, write missing proofs or Mathlib anchors. Recurse into anything new you introduce.
110	5. For any block needing a source you don't have locally, spawn a `reference-retriever`, wait, read the file, then write the cited block.
111	6. Re-query the cone; fix residual broken edges / ∞ holes until only genuine gaps remain.
112	7. Write your report.
113	
114	## Logging
115	
116	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/dag-walker-<slug>.md` (or the nested `task_results/<parent-slug>/...` path your invocation names).
117	
118	```markdown
119	# DAG Walker Report
120	
121	## Slug
122	<slug>
123	
124	## Seed
125	<label>
126	
127	## Status
128	<COMPLETE — cone fully grounded | PARTIAL — genuine gaps remain (listed below)>
129	
130	## Cone before → after
131	- ∞ holes: <N before> → <M after>
132	- broken \uses: <N before> → <M after>
133	- blocks added: <count>;  \uses edges added: <count>
134	
135	## Blocks added / proofs written
136	- `\label{...}` in `chapters/<slug>.tex` — <one line: statement / why it was a missing dependency>
137	- Proof sketch for `<label>` — <brief shape; ∞ → finite>
138	- Mathlib anchor `<label>` → `\lean{<real Mathlib decl>}` `\mathlibok`
139	
140	## \uses edges added/fixed (the completeness fixes)
141	- `<node>` now `\uses{<dep>}` — its proof invokes <dep> but didn't declare it (made the frontier honest).
142	- Fixed broken `\uses{<bad>}` in `<node>` → `<good>` / created `<dep>`.
143	
144	## Could not complete (genuine gaps — strategy items)
145	- `<label>` — no informal proof exists (hard / novel / not in literature). Left ∞. <strategy implication, if any>
146	
147	## References consulted
148	- `references/<file>.md` — <what verbatim content you took>
149	
150	## Notes for dispatcher
151	- <cross-chapter concerns, needed macros, oversized chapters, etc.>
152	```
153	
154	## Return Value
155	
156	Your final assistant message: one line — `<slug>: COMPLETE | PARTIAL — <one-sentence outcome>` — plus the path to your full report. Keep it short; the dispatcher reads the report.

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.

52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |

70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116	
117	## Agent Roles
118	
119	### Plan Agent
120	
121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
122	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
123	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
124	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
125	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
126	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
127	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
128	
129	### Prover Agent
130	
131	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
132	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
133	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
134	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
135	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
136	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
137	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
138	
139	### Subagents
140	
141	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
142	
143	### Review Agent
144	
145	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
146	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
147	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
148	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
149	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
150	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
151	
152	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
153	
154	These run automatically between phases:
155	
156	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
157	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
158	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
159

1	\chapter{Rigidity for morphisms of schemes (scheme-level form; Mumford \S4 in the abelian-variety case)}
2	\label{chap:Rigidity}
3	
4	This chapter records a self-contained helper: \emph{rigidity for morphisms of schemes that agree on a non-empty open}. The classical motivation is Mumford's rigidity for abelian-variety morphisms, but the project's formalised statement is more general: the source-side group-object hypothesis is unused by the proof and is dropped.
5	
6	The lemma is independent of the construction of \(\Jac(C)\). It is the load-bearing input for the M2.a sub-step of the genus-0 Albanese-witness argument (Jacobian.tex \S C.2.b), where the source is taken to be \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) (which is \emph{not} a group scheme).
7	
8	\section{Statement}
9	
10	\begin{theorem}\leanok
11	[Rigidity for morphisms of schemes (scheme-level form)]
12	  \label{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen}
13	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Over.ext_of_eqOnOpen}
14	  Let \(k\) be a field. Let \(X\) be a reduced geometrically irreducible \(k\)-scheme and let \(Y\) be a separated \(k\)-scheme. Let \(g_1, g_2 \colon X \to Y\) be two morphisms of \(k\)-schemes, and let \(U \subseteq X\) be a non-empty open subset. If
15	  \[
16	    U.\iota \circ g_1 \;=\; U.\iota \circ g_2
17	  \]
18	  as scheme morphisms (where \(U.\iota \colon U \hookrightarrow X\) is the canonical open immersion), then \(g_1 = g_2\) as morphisms of \(k\)-schemes.
19	\end{theorem}
20	
21	\begin{remark}
22	  The classical Mumford rigidity theorem (\emph{Abelian Varieties}, \S4) usually states the special case where \(Y\) is an abelian variety and the conclusion is phrased as ``a morphism vanishing on a non-empty open is zero''. The two forms are interderivable in the abelian-variety case via the difference morphism \(\delta = g_1 \cdot g_2^{-1}\) formed from the group law on \(Y\). The project formalises the more general statement above because the M2.a (genus-0 Albanese-witness) consumer specifically needs the source to be \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\), which is not a group scheme: the abelian-variety packaging is consumer-side and the scheme-level statement is what the proof actually establishes.
23	
24	  The refactor that landed the scheme-level form (iter-125; see \texttt{analogies/rigidity-refactor.md}) dropped 8 unused hypotheses from the project's original group-scheme-flavoured declaration and weakened the target-side \([\mathtt{IsProper}\ Y.\mathrm{hom}]\) to \([\mathtt{IsSeparated}\ Y.\mathrm{hom}]\).
25	\end{remark}
26	
27	\section{Proof sketch}
28	
29	\begin{proof}
30	  \leanok
31	  We reduce to Mathlib's \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Separated}), which gives the standard scheme-level fact that two morphisms from a reduced scheme to a separated scheme agreeing on a dominant subscheme agree everywhere.
32	
33	  The three load-bearing instances feeding that closure are:
34	  \begin{itemize}
35	    \item \emph{\(Y \to \Spec k\) is separated.} Hypothesis \([\mathtt{IsSeparated}\ Y.\mathrm{hom}]\) provides this directly (no projection from \(\mathtt{IsProper}\) needed). The \texttt{OverClass.fromOver} instance transports it to the form \(\mathtt{IsSeparated}\ (Y.\mathrm{left} \downarrow \Spec k)\) that the closure step expects.
36	    \item \emph{\(X\) is an irreducible space.} Hypothesis \([\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}\ X.\mathrm{hom}]\) over the field-spectrum base \(\Spec k\) (which is a single point, hence subsingleton) yields \(\mathtt{IrreducibleSpace}\ X.\mathrm{left}\) via \texttt{GeometricallyIrreducible.irreducibleSpace\_of\_subsingleton}.
37	    \item \emph{The inclusion \(U.\iota\) is dominant.} A non-empty open in an irreducible space is dense, and a dense open immersion is dominant: \texttt{Scheme.PartialMap.Opens.isDominant\_ι}.
38	  \end{itemize}
39	  With these three derived instances and the hypothesis \([\mathtt{IsReduced}\ X.\mathrm{left}]\), the closure \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} produces the underlying scheme-morphism equality \(g_1.\mathrm{left} = g_2.\mathrm{left}\) from the agreement-on-\(U\) hypothesis. The promotion to \(g_1 = g_2\) in \(\mathrm{Over}\ (\Spec k)\) uses \texttt{Over.OverMorphism.ext}.
40	
41	  \medskip
42	  \noindent\textbf{Mathlib ingredients.}
43	  \begin{itemize}
44	    \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} for the scheme-level closure step.
45	    \item \texttt{GeometricallyIrreducible.irreducibleSpace\_of\_subsingleton} to derive irreducibility of \(X.\mathrm{left}\).
46	    \item \texttt{Scheme.PartialMap.Opens.isDominant\_ι} to derive dominance of the open immersion \(U.\iota\) from the non-empty hypothesis.
47	    \item \texttt{Over.OverMorphism.ext} to promote underlying scheme equality to \(\mathrm{Over}\)-morphism equality.
48	    \item No abelian-variety / group-object infrastructure is needed: smoothness, properness, and group-object hypotheses on \(X\) and \(Y\) are all dropped from the formalised statement.
49	  \end{itemize}
50	\end{proof}
51	
52	\section{Use in the project}
53	
54	The rigidity theorem is used in two places downstream:
55	
56	\begin{itemize}
57	  \item \textbf{M2.a (genus-0 Albanese witness).} Jacobian.tex \S C.2.b reduces the genus-0 rigidity-over-\(\bar k\) argument (for \(f \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A_{\bar k}\) hitting the identity at a \(\bar k\)-point) to this theorem with \(X := \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) and \(Y := A_{\bar k}\). The refactor of iter-125 (dropping the source-side group-object hypothesis) is what enables this application. The named project declaration packaging the C.2.a–C.2.e reduction is \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} in \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}; that chapter scaffolds the iter-126 Lean target \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity\_over\_kbar} with the C.2.d cotangent-vanishing keystone residual.
58	  \item \textbf{Uniqueness in the Albanese property.} Used in the uniqueness half of Theorem~REF (the Albanese property of \(\Jac(C)\)): given two morphisms \(g_1, g_2 \colon \Jac(C) \to A\) with \(g_1 \circ \alpha_P = g_2 \circ \alpha_P\), scheme-level agreement on the image of \(\alpha_P\) (a non-empty open after passing to \(C^{(g)} \twoheadrightarrow \Jac(C)\)) forces \(g_1 = g_2\).
59	\end{itemize}
60	
61	Note: the existence half of REF requires Phase~B/C infrastructure (Picard scheme, dual abelian variety). Rigidity alone yields \emph{uniqueness} only.
62	
63	\section{Mathlib status}
64	
65	The scheme-level form is a thin specialization of Mathlib's existing \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} (in \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Separated}); the project's contribution is the \(\mathrm{Over}\ (\Spec k)\)-bundled packaging plus the convenience derivations of \texttt{IrreducibleSpace} and \texttt{IsDominant} from the project-side hypotheses.
66	
67	An upstream-PR candidate is a thin corollary in Mathlib's \texttt{Separated} file:
68	\[
69	  \texttt{ext\_of\_eqOnNonemptyOpen} \;\colon\; \texttt{(non-empty open) + (reduced source) + (separated target) }\Rightarrow\texttt{ ext}
70	\]
71	packaging the dense-and-dominant chain into a single API call. The incremental value over the existing \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} is small (just the API convenience of "non-empty open" over "dominant morphism"); see \texttt{analogies/rigidity-refactor.md} for the proposed statement and home file. The project does not pursue this upstream PR as part of the iter-125 work.
72

1	\chapter{Cotangent space at the identity (piece (i) --- Lean file)}
2	\label{chap:cotangent-grpobj}
3	
4	This chapter is a pointer to the Lean file
5	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Cotangent/GrpObj.lean}. The mathematical content
6	itself lives in chapter~REF (\texttt{RigidityKbar.tex}
7	\S "Piece (i)") and is not duplicated here. This chapter exists to satisfy
8	the per-Lean-file blueprint convention (one chapter per Lean file).
9	
10	\textbf{Post-iter-145 disposition.} After the iter-145 refactor
11	\texttt{refactor-chart-algebra-skeleton-bundled-excise-iter145}, the
12	file contains the piece (i.a) trio
13	(\texttt{cotangentSpaceAtIdentity},
14	\texttt{cotangentSpaceAtIdentity\_eq\_extendScalars},
15	\texttt{cotangentSpaceAtIdentity\_finrank\_eq}, all closed
16	iter-128--iter-132) plus a small set of orphan helpers from the
17	iter-134--iter-136 bundled-route build
18	(\texttt{shearMulRight} with its \texttt{\_hom\_fst}/\texttt{\_hom\_snd}
19	companions, \texttt{schemeHomRingCompatibility}, and
20	\texttt{relativeDifferentialsPresheaf\_restrict\_along\_identity\_section}),
21	together with two private structural helpers
22	(\texttt{section\_snd\_eq\_identity\_struct} and
23	\texttt{isIso\_of\_app\_iso\_module}) that the surviving public
24	declarations use locally. Zero \texttt{sorry}-bodied declarations
25	remain in this file. The five excised bundled-route declarations
26	(\texttt{basechange\_along\_proj\_two\_inv\_derivation},
27	\texttt{basechange\_along\_proj\_two\_inv},
28	\texttt{basechange\_along\_proj\_two\_inv\_app\_isIso},
29	\texttt{relativeDifferentialsPresheaf\_basechange\_along\_proj\_two},
30	\texttt{mulRight\_globalises\_cotangent}) and their iter-138--iter-143
31	build history live in the git history of this file (per the iter-145
32	Q7 "auditable record IS git history" framing in \texttt{STRATEGY.md}
33	\S Iter-144 chart-algebra pivot). The active chart-algebra piece (ii)
34	work has moved to \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Cotangent/ChartAlgebra.lean}
35	with informal content in \cref{chap:RigidityKbar} \S "Chart-algebra
36	piece (ii) first-class decomposition" of \texttt{RigidityKbar.tex}.
37	
38	\section{Lean declarations in this file}
39	
40	\begin{itemize}
41	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GrpObj.cotangentSpaceAtIdentity}

content: Now I have the structural map. Let me read the key sections of AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex — the rigidity lemma section and the headline/P1 spine.

78	\section{The Rigidity Lemma}
79	\begin{lemma}
80	\leanok
81	[Rigidity Lemma (Mumford, Form I)]
82	  \label{thm:rigidity_lemma}
83	  \uses{lem:rigidity_eqOn_dense_open}
84	  
85	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_lemma}
86	  % SOURCE: [Mumford, Abelian Varieties], Rigidity Lemma (Form I.), Ch.~II \S4, book p.~43 (read from references/mumford-abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 54)
87	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "RIGIDITY LEMMA. (Form I.) Let \(X\) be a complete variety, \(Y\) and \(Z\)
88	  % any varieties, and \(f \colon X \times Y \to Z\) a morphism such that for some
89	  % \(y_0 \in Y\), \(f(X \times \{y_0\})\) is a single point \(z_0\) of \(Z\). Then there is a
90	  % morphism \(g \colon Y \to Z\) such that if \(p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y\) is the
91	  % projection, \(f = g \circ p_2\)."
92	  \textit{Source: Mumford, Abelian Varieties, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma (Form~I), p.~43.}
93	  Let \(X\) be a complete variety, and let \(Y\), \(Z\) be any varieties over \(\bar k\). Let
94	  \(f \colon X \times Y \to Z\) be a morphism such that for some \(y_0 \in Y\) the image
95	  \(f(X \times \{y_0\})\) is a single point \(z_0 \in Z\). Then there is a morphism
96	  \(g \colon Y \to Z\) with \(f = g \circ p_2\), where \(p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y\) is the
97	  projection; that is, \(f\) depends only on the \(Y\)-coordinate, and \(g(y) = f(x_0, y)\) for any
98	  fixed \(x_0 \in X\).
99	\end{lemma}
100	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. Choose any point \(x_0 \in X\), and define \(g \colon Y \to Z\) by
101	% \(g(y) = f(x_0, y)\). Since \(X \times Y\) is a variety, to show that \(f = g \circ p_2\), it is
102	% sufficient to show that these morphisms coincide on some open subset of \(X \times Y\). Let
103	% \(U\) be an affine open neighbourhood of \(z_0\) in \(Z\), \(F = Z - U\), and \(G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))\);
104	% then \(G\) is closed in \(Y\) since \(X\) is complete and hence \(p_2\) is a closed map. Further
105	% \(y_0 \notin G\) since \(f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\}\). Therefore \(Y - G = V\) is a non-empty
106	% open subset of \(Y\). For each \(y \in V\), the complete variety \(X \times \{y\}\) gets mapped by
107	% \(f\) into the affine variety \(U\), and hence to a single point of \(U\). But this means that for
108	% any \(x \in X\), \(y \in V\), \(f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g \circ p_2(x, y)\), and this proves our
109	% assertion."
110	\begin{proof}
111	\leanok
112	% NOTE (iter-159 review): the iter-157 unsoundness REMAINS REPAIRED. The collapse hypothesis
113	% (_hf : f(X x {y0}) = {z0}) is threaded through all three helpers (rigidity_lemma ->
114	% rigidity_core -> rigidity_eqOn_dense_open) and GENUINELY CONSUMED in the body of
115	% rigidity_eqOn_dense_open (proves y0 \notin G, the load-bearing non-emptiness of V := Y - G).
116	% The counterexample f = fst is excluded (under f = fst, _hf forces X to collapse to a point).
117	% rigidity_eqOn_dense_open is sorry-free in its OWN body (hfib, the pullback-fibre fact over the
118	% k-bar-point y0, is closed). rigidity_snd_lift and snd_left_isClosedMap are axiom-clean (no sorryAx).
119	% NOTE (iter-160): the \uses edges run FORWARD along the true Lean dependency order
120	% (rigidity_lemma -> rigidity_eqOn_dense_open -> rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine), so the
121	% not-proven status of the residual sorry propagates up and de-launders BOTH thm:rigidity_lemma and
122	% lem:rigidity_eqOn_dense_open. No backward edge from the leaf lemmas back up the chain.
123	% NOTE (iter-161): bridge 2 (rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine) was DECOMPOSED into two named
124	% top-level sub-lemmas along the route-B (cohomology-free) split:
125	%   - Step 2, morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints: "two morphisms agreeing at every closed point of a
126	%     reduced Jacobson source into a separated target are equal" -- PROVEN axiom-clean (the
127	%     dense-closed-points coproduct probe + ext_of_isDominant; the connective Mathlib lacks).
128	%   - Step 1, rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine: Mumford's per-closed-slice constancy
129	%     -- the genuinely-deep residual, still `sorry`. THIS is now the chain's lone deep residual
130	%     (bridge 2's body is real assembly: Step 2 proven, the JacobsonSpace U instance now derivable).
131	% SIGNATURE CHANGE (iter-161, plan-authorized): Step 2's globalisation needs the closed points of
132	% the saturated open U to be DENSE, i.e. U Jacobson, which over Spec k-bar follows from (X tensor Y)
133	% being LOCALLY OF FINITE TYPE. So [LocallyOfFiniteType (X tensor Y).hom] is now carried on FIVE
134	% chain lemmas (rigidity_lemma, rigidity_core, rigidity_eqOn_dense_open,
135	% rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine, rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine), in
136	% addition to [IsAlgClosed kbar]. Free downstream: curves and abelian varieties are of finite type
137	% over k-bar. The earlier "[IsAlgClosed] is the only added instance" claim is hereby retired.
138	  Fix any point \(x_0 \in X\) and define \(g \colon Y \to Z\) by \(g(y) = f(x_0, y)\) (the
139	  composite of \(y \mapsto (x_0, y)\) with \(f\)). Since \(X \times Y\) is a variety (in
140	  particular reduced and irreducible, so separated and with no embedded structure), to prove
141	  the equality of morphisms \(f = g \circ p_2\) it suffices to prove they agree on a non-empty
142	  open subset of \(X \times Y\).
143	  Let \(U\) be an affine open neighbourhood of \(z_0\) in \(Z\), put \(F = Z - U\) (a closed set),
144	  and set \(G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))\). Because \(X\) is complete, the projection
145	  \(p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y\) is a \emph{closed} map (this is the defining property of
146	  completeness/properness); hence \(G\), the image of the closed set \(f^{-1}(F)\), is closed in
147	  \(Y\). The hypothesis \(f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\} \subseteq U\) gives
148	  \(f^{-1}(F) \cap (X \times \{y_0\}) = \varnothing\), so \(y_0 \notin G\). Therefore
149	  \(V := Y - G\) is a non-empty open subset of \(Y\).
150	  For each \(y \in V\) we have \(X \times \{y\} \cap f^{-1}(F) = \varnothing\), i.e.
151	  \(f(X \times \{y\}) \subseteq U\). Thus the complete (proper) variety \(X \times \{y\}\) is
152	  mapped by \(f\) into the \emph{affine} variety \(U\). A proper connected variety mapping to an
153	  affine variety has image a single point (a global regular function on a complete connected
154	  variety is constant). Hence \(f(X \times \{y\})\) is one point, necessarily
155	  \(f(x_0, y) = g(y)\). So for all \(x \in X\) and \(y \in V\),
156	  \[ f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g(y) = (g \circ p_2)(x, y). \]
157	  The two morphisms \(f\) and \(g \circ p_2\) thus agree on the non-empty open
158	  \(X \times V\), which is dense in the irreducible \(X \times Y\); as \(Z\) is separated they
159	  agree everywhere. \qedhere
160	\end{proof}
161	\begin{remark}
162	  \label{rmk:rigidity_lemma_cube_free}
163	  The proof of \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma} uses \emph{only} (i) that completeness of \(X\) makes
164	  \(p_2\) a closed map, and (ii) that a proper connected variety has no nonconstant map to an
165	  affine variety. It uses neither the theorem of the cube nor any cohomology, and is valid in
166	  arbitrary characteristic. This is the designated formalisation entry point: it is the
167	  lowest-prerequisite link of the chain. (Mumford gives this as the geometric heart of the
168	  ``second proof'' of commutativity of an abelian variety; Milne packages the same statement
169	  as the Rigidity Theorem~1.1, requiring \(V\) complete and \(V \times W\) geometrically
170	  irreducible.)
171	\end{remark}
172	\begin{remark}
173	  \label{rmk:rigidity_lemma_decomposition}
174	  The Lean formalisation of \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma} splits into three helper steps, mirroring
175	  the proof above: \texttt{rigidity\_snd\_lift} (the cartesian-monoidal algebra identifying the
176	  collapsed map \((x, y) \mapsto (x_0, y)\) followed by \(f\) with \(g \circ p_2\));
177	  \texttt{rigidity\_core} (the scheme-level gluing: two maps out of the geometrically irreducible
178	  reduced \(X \times Y\) into the separated \(Z\) that agree on a non-empty open subset agree
179	  everywhere, by separatedness); and \texttt{rigidity\_eqOn\_dense\_open}
180	  (\cref{lem:rigidity_eqOn_dense_open}, the genuine geometry: the construction of the non-empty
181	  open \(X \times V\) and the slice-constancy of \(f\) on it). The collapse hypothesis
182	  \(f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\}\) must be threaded through \emph{all three} helpers, since it is
183	  what makes \(V\) non-empty; a decomposition that drops it from the lower helpers is unsound
184	  (see \cref{lem:rigidity_eqOn_dense_open}).
185	  \emph{Status (iter-161).} Bridge~1 (the closed-map step) is \textbf{built} as the axiom-clean
186	  helper \texttt{snd\_left\_isClosedMap}. The fibre-over-\(y_0\) fact is \textbf{closed} (via the
187	  coarse \texttt{PullbackCarrier} layer), so \texttt{rigidity\_eqOn\_dense\_open} is
188	  \texttt{sorry}-free in its own body. Bridge~2 (slice-constancy on the saturated open \(U\),
189	  \cref{lem:rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine}) has now been \textbf{decomposed} along its
190	  cohomology-free route-(c) split into two named sub-lemmas: the globalisation
191	  \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints} (Step~2, ``agree at every closed point of a reduced
192	  Jacobson source \(\Rightarrow\) equal'') is \textbf{proven axiom-clean}, and the per-closed-slice
193	  constancy \cref{lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine} (Step~1, Mumford's slice
194	  argument) is the chain's \emph{single genuinely-deep residual} \texttt{sorry}. The body of
195	  \cref{lem:rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine} is therefore real assembly (Step~2 over Step~1).
196	  % NOTE: (iter-162 review) STALE as of iter-162 — Step~1 is now PROVEN and the WHOLE
197	  % Rigidity-Lemma chain (thm:rigidity_lemma … lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine,
198	  % via the new helper lem:isIntegral_of_retract_of_integral) is sorry-free + axiom-clean
199	  % ({propext, Classical.choice, Quot.sound}, re-verified). The "single genuinely-deep residual
200	  % sorry" wording here and in the proof blocks of lem:rigidity_eqOn_dense_open / …saturated… /
201	  % …eqAt_closedPoint… should be refreshed by the plan/blueprint-writer to "chain closed iter-162".
202	  This route requires the base field to be algebraically closed \emph{and} the source to be of finite
203	  type: Step~2 needs the closed points of \(U\) to be dense (the Jacobson-space property), which over
204	  the algebraically closed \(\bar k\) holds because \(X \times Y\) is locally of finite type.
205	  Accordingly the formalization carries \textbf{both} \([\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]\) and
206	  \([\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]\) across the chain lemmas
207	  (\texttt{rigidity\_lemma}, \texttt{rigidity\_core}, \texttt{rigidity\_eqOn\_dense\_open},
208	  \texttt{rigidity\_eqOn\_saturated\_open\_to\_affine},
209	  \texttt{rigidity\_eqAt\_closedPoint\_of\_proper\_into\_affine}); the earlier claim that
210	  \([\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]\) is the \emph{only} added instance is retired. Both cost nothing
211	  downstream: curves and abelian varieties are of finite type over the algebraically closed \(\bar k\),
212	  and the consumers already assume it. The relative Stein-factorisation / proper-pushforward
213	  \(f_*\mathcal O = \mathcal O\) framing is a genuine Mathlib cohomology gap and is \emph{deliberately
214	  avoided}.
215	\end{remark}
216	\begin{lemma}
217	\leanok
218	[Dense-open agreement: the geometric heart of the Rigidity Lemma]
219	  \label{lem:rigidity_eqOn_dense_open}
220	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_eqOn_dense_open}
221	  % SOURCE: [Mumford, Abelian Varieties], Rigidity Lemma (Form I.), Ch.~II \S4, book p.~43 (read from references/mumford-abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 54)
222	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "RIGIDITY LEMMA. (Form I.) Let \(X\) be a complete variety, \(Y\) and \(Z\)
223	  % any varieties, and \(f \colon X \times Y \to Z\) a morphism such that for some
224	  % \(y_0 \in Y\), \(f(X \times \{y_0\})\) is a single point \(z_0\) of \(Z\). Then there is a
225	  % morphism \(g \colon Y \to Z\) such that if \(p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y\) is the
226	  % projection, \(f = g \circ p_2\)."
227	  \textit{Source: Mumford, Abelian Varieties, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma (Form~I), p.~43 (the
228	  dense-open construction is the body of that proof).}
229	  Let \(X\) be a complete variety and \(Y\), \(Z\) any varieties over \(\bar k\). Let
230	  \(f \colon X \times Y \to Z\) be a morphism, fix a point \(x_0 \in X\), and suppose given
231	  \(y_0 \in Y\), \(z_0 \in Z\) with the \emph{collapse hypothesis}
232	  \[ f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\} \qquad
233	     \text{(in Lean: } \mathtt{lift}\,(\mathbf 1_X)\,(\mathtt{toUnit}\,X \fatsemi y_0) \fatsemi f
234	     = \mathtt{toUnit}\,X \fatsemi z_0\text{).} \]
235	  Then there is a non-empty open subset \(U \subseteq X \times Y\) on which \(f\) agrees, as a
236	  scheme morphism, with the collapsed map \(\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f\), where
237	  $\mathtt{retract} := \mathtt{lift}\,(\mathtt{toUnit}\,(X \times Y) \fatsemi x_0)\,
238	  (p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y)\( is the ``\)(x, y) \mapsto (x_0, y)$'' endomorphism. Concretely
239	  \(U = X \times V\) with \(V := Y - G\), \(G = p_2(f^{-1}(Z - U_0))\) for an affine open
240	  neighbourhood \(U_0\) of \(z_0\), and on \(U\) one has \(f(x, y) = f(x_0, y)\).
241	  \medskip
242	  \noindent\textbf{The collapse hypothesis is load-bearing.} The non-emptiness of \(V\) is exactly
243	  the assertion \(y_0 \notin G\), and this holds \emph{because} $f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\}
244	  \subseteq U_0\( keeps the slice over \)y_0\( inside the affine \)U_0\(, away from \)F = Z - U_0$. A
245	  version of this lemma \emph{without} the collapse hypothesis is false: for \(X = Y = Z\) and
246	  \(f = p_1\) the first projection, \(f\) and \(\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f \colon (x, y) \mapsto x_0\)
247	  agree on no non-empty open (the agreement locus is contained in \(\{x = x_0\}\), which has empty
248	  interior in the irreducible \(X \times Y\)). Therefore the formal target \emph{must} carry the
249	  collapse hypothesis as an explicit antecedent; dropping it (as an earlier decomposition
250	  silently did) makes the deferred goal unsatisfiable.
251	  \medskip
252	  \noindent\textbf{Formalization note: algebraically closed base, and locally-of-finite-type
253	  source.} The prose already takes \(\bar k\) algebraically closed, but the formal statement must
254	  \emph{encode} two properties. First, the cohomology-free proof of the slice-constancy bridge
255	  (below) needs each per-closed-point residue field \(\kappa(y)\) to be \(\bar k\) itself, so that a
256	  proper integral slice has global sections \(\Gamma = \bar k\) and maps to a single \(\bar k\)-point of
257	  the affine \(U_0\); this requires \([\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]\). Second, the globalisation of the
258	  per-slice agreement (Step~2 of the bridge, \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}) needs the
259	  closed points of the saturated open \(U\) to be \emph{dense} --- i.e.\ \(U\) to be a Jacobson space.
260	  Over the algebraically closed \(\bar k\) this holds precisely because \(X \times Y\) is \emph{locally of
261	  finite type} over \(\bar k\) (\texttt{LocallyOfFiniteType.jacobsonSpace} transports the Jacobson
262	  property of \(\Spec \bar k\), and \texttt{closure\_closedPoints} then gives density); this requires
263	  \([\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]\). Accordingly the formalization carries
264	  \emph{both} \([\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]\) and
265	  \([\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]\) on this lemma (in addition to
266	  \([\mathtt{Field}\ \bar k]\)), and propagates the same two instances to \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma},
267	  its core helper \texttt{rigidity\_core}, and the bridge sub-lemmas
268	  \cref{lem:rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine} and
269	  \cref{lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine}. Both cost nothing downstream: the
270	  consumers \cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant} and \cref{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} (Lean
271	  \texttt{rigidity\_genus0\_curve\_to\_grpScheme}) \emph{already} assume
272	  \([\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]\) --- the base-field variable is literally named \(\mathtt{kbar}\) ---
273	  and curves and abelian varieties are of finite type over \(\bar k\), so the
274	  locally-of-finite-type instance is automatic there.
275	\end{lemma}
276	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. Choose any point \(x_0 \in X\), and define \(g \colon Y \to Z\) by
277	% \(g(y) = f(x_0, y)\). Since \(X \times Y\) is a variety, to show that \(f = g \circ p_2\), it is
278	% sufficient to show that these morphisms coincide on some open subset of \(X \times Y\). Let
279	% \(U\) be an affine open neighbourhood of \(z_0\) in \(Z\), \(F = Z - U\), and \(G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))\);
280	% then \(G\) is closed in \(Y\) since \(X\) is complete and hence \(p_2\) is a closed map. Further
281	% \(y_0 \notin G\) since \(f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\}\). Therefore \(Y - G = V\) is a non-empty
282	% open subset of \(Y\). For each \(y \in V\), the complete variety \(X \times \{y\}\) gets mapped by
283	% \(f\) into the affine variety \(U\), and hence to a single point of \(U\). But this means that for
284	% any \(x \in X\), \(y \in V\), \(f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g \circ p_2(x, y)\), and this proves our
285	% assertion."
286	\begin{proof}
287	  \leanok
288	  Let \(U_0\) be an affine open neighbourhood of \(z_0\) in \(Z\), put \(F = Z - U_0\), and set
289	  \(G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))\). Because \(X\) is complete, the projection \(p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y\) is a
290	  \emph{closed} map, so \(G\) (the image of the closed set \(f^{-1}(F)\)) is closed in \(Y\). The
291	  collapse hypothesis \(f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\} \subseteq U_0\) gives
292	  \(f^{-1}(F) \cap (X \times \{y_0\}) = \varnothing\), hence \(y_0 \notin G\); this is the only place
293	  the collapse hypothesis is used, and it is essential, for \(V := Y - G\) is non-empty precisely
294	  because \(y_0 \in V\). For each \(y \in V\) the proper variety \(X \times \{y\}\) is mapped by \(f\)
295	  into the affine \(U_0\), hence to a single point, necessarily \(f(x_0, y)\). So for all \(x \in X\),
296	  \(y \in V\), we have \(f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)(x, y)\), i.e.\ \(f\) and
297	  \(\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f\) agree on the non-empty open \(U := X \times V\). (In the Lean
298	  encoding the two slice-constancy bridges are the closed-map step from properness and the
299	  ``proper-into-affine has single-point image'' step; see the docstring of
300	  \texttt{rigidity\_core}.)
301	  \medskip
302	  \noindent\textbf{Formalization notes (the concrete char-free Mathlib route).} The textbook
303	  proof above is realised in Lean by three pieces. They cite Mathlib lemma names as the chosen
304	  route; none uses cohomology, and the relative Stein-factorisation framing is deliberately
305	  \emph{avoided} (see below).
306	  \emph{Bridge~1 (the closed-map step) --- BUILT.} ``\(X\) complete \(\Rightarrow p_2\) closed'' is
307	  the axiom-clean helper \texttt{snd\_left\_isClosedMap} (iter-158). It is obtained from
308	  \texttt{IsProper.toUniversallyClosed} on \(X\), base-changed across the canonical pullback square

310	  pullback projection: \texttt{Over.snd\_left} rewrites \((\mathtt{snd}\,X\,Y).\mathtt{left}\) to
311	  \(\mathtt{Limits.pullback.snd}\ X.\mathtt{hom}\ Y.\mathtt{hom}\) exactly. This step is already
312	  proven.
313	  \emph{The fibre fact (non-emptiness of \(V\)) --- RESOLVED route, char-free.} That the slice
314	  \(X \times \{y_0\}\) over the \(\bar k\)-rational point \(y_0\) is exactly the image of the slice

324	  \([\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}]\).

326	  ``Proper connected slice into affine \(\Rightarrow\) single point, glued to a morphism equality on
327	  \(U\)'' is realised by a \textbf{global-sections + per-closed-point} route, \emph{not} by any
328	  relative Stein-factorisation or proper-pushforward \(f_*\mathcal O = \mathcal O\) statement: the
329	  latter is a genuine Mathlib gap (no Stein factorisation, no \(H^0\) base change), and route~(c)

332	  the slice \(X_y\) is proper integral, so its global sections form a field
333	  (\texttt{isField\_of\_universallyClosed}) module-finite over \(\bar k\)

337	  \(\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f\) agree on each closed slice. Closed points are dense in the

350	  (\cref{lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine}); Step~1 (Mumford's per-closed-slice

376	  ``for each \(y \in V\), the complete slice maps into the affine, hence to a single point'' step).}
377	  Let \(\bar k\) be algebraically closed and let \(X\), \(Y\), \(Z\) be objects of
378	  \(\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec \bar k)\) with \(X\) proper, \(X \times Y\) geometrically irreducible, reduced,
379	  and \emph{locally of finite type} over \(\bar k\) (the formalization carries
380	  \([\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]\), which makes the saturated open \(U\)
381	  below a Jacobson space, so its closed points are dense --- the hypothesis Step~2 of the proof
382	  consumes), and \(Z\) separated. Let \(f \colon X \times Y \to Z\) be a morphism, fix a \(\bar k\)-point
383	  \(x_0 \colon \mathbf 1 \to X\), and write $\mathtt{retract} := \mathtt{lift}\,
384	  (\mathtt{toUnit}\,(X \times Y) \fatsemi x_0)\,(p_2)\( for the ``\)(x, y) \mapsto (x_0, y)$''
385	  endomorphism. Suppose:
386	  \begin{itemize}
387	    \item \(U \subseteq X \times Y\) is a \(p_2\)-\emph{saturated} open, i.e.\ \(U = p_2^{-1}(V_{\mathrm{set}})\)
388	      for a set \(V_{\mathrm{set}} \subseteq Y\) (so \(U\) contains the whole fibre \(X_y\) over each of
389	      its points);
390	    \item \(U_0 \subseteq Z\) is an \emph{affine} open;
391	    \item \(f(U) \subseteq U_0\), i.e.\ \(f\) maps \(U\) into the affine \(U_0\).
392	  \end{itemize}

394	  \(U.\iota \fatsemi f = U.\iota \fatsemi (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)\).

396	\begin{proof}
397	  \leanok
398	  This is the formalization realization of Mumford's step ``for each \(y \in V\), the complete
399	  variety \(X \times \{y\}\) gets mapped by \(f\) into the affine variety \(U\), and hence to a single
400	  point of \(U\)''. We realise it by a \textbf{global-sections + per-closed-point} route that is
401	  \emph{cohomology-free}; in particular the relative Stein-factorisation / proper-pushforward
402	  \(f_*\mathcal O = \mathcal O\) framing is a confirmed Mathlib gap (no Stein factorisation, no
403	  \(H^0\) base change) and is \emph{deliberately avoided}. The argument splits into two named
404	  sub-lemmas, one residual and one proven.

407	  Fix a closed point \(x\) of \(U\), lying over a closed point \(y = p_2(x) \in V_{\mathrm{set}}\).
408	  Since \(\bar k\) is algebraically closed and \(Y\) is of finite type over \(\bar k\), the residue field
409	  is \(\kappa(y) = \bar k\). Because \(U = p_2^{-1}(V_{\mathrm{set}})\) is saturated, \(U\) contains the
410	  \emph{entire} fibre \(X_y \cong X\) over \(y\); hence \(f\) maps the proper integral slice \(X_y\) into the
411	  affine \(U_0\). By \(\mathtt{isField\_of\_universallyClosed}\) the global sections \(\Gamma(X_y)\) form a
412	  field, module-finite over \(\bar k\) by \(\mathtt{finite\_appTop\_of\_universallyClosed}\), and
413	  algebraic closedness collapses the finite extension to give \(\Gamma(X_y) = \bar k\). A morphism into
414	  the affine \(U_0\) is pinned by its ring map on global sections (\(\mathtt{ext\_of\_isAffine}\)), so the
415	  restriction of \(f\) to \(X_y\) factors through a single \(\bar k\)-point of \(U_0\) --- necessarily the
416	  point \(f(x_0, y) = (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)(x)\). Thus \(f\) and \(\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f\)
417	  agree (after the residue-field probe) at every closed point of \(U\). This per-slice statement is the
418	  chain's single genuinely-deep residual \texttt{sorry}.
419	  \medskip
420	  \noindent\textbf{Step 2 (globalisation over dense closed points) --- \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}, proven.}
421	  The closed points are dense in the locally-of-finite-type \(\bar k\)-scheme \(U\)
422	  (\(\mathtt{closure\_closedPoints}\) / the Jacobson-space property, supplied here by the new hypothesis
423	  \([\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]\)). To turn ``\(f\) and
424	  \(\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f\) agree at each closed point'' into a single morphism equality on \(U\),
425	  the proven globalisation \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints} assembles the closed points
426	  into one dominant probe --- the coproduct
427	  \(\coprod_{x \in \mathrm{closedPoints}\,U} \Spec \kappa(x) \to U\), whose range is dense --- and feeds
428	  it to the reduced-source / separated-target rigidity handle \(\mathtt{ext\_of\_isDominant}\) (the same
429	  family \(\mathtt{rigidity\_core}\) uses). This yields the morphism equality
430	  \(U.\iota \fatsemi f = U.\iota \fatsemi (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)\) on all of \(U\). The
431	  ``agrees on a dense set of closed points \(\Rightarrow\) hom-equality'' connective is the one piece
432	  Mathlib does not package directly (it supplies only the single-dominant-morphism
433	  \(\mathtt{ext\_of\_isDominant}\)); that bespoke build is now done in
434	  \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}, and it is \emph{not} cohomology. \qedhere
435	\end{proof}
436	\begin{lemma}\leanok
437	[Step~2: morphisms agreeing at every closed point of a Jacobson source are equal]
438	  \label{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}
439	  
440	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}
441	  % Project-bespoke assembly: NO external source. Mathlib supplies only the single-dominant-morphism
442	  % ext_of_isDominant; the "dense closed points => hom-extensionality" connective is built here.
443	  Let \(W\), \(Z\) be schemes with \(W\) \emph{reduced}, \(W\) a \emph{Jacobson space}, and \(Z\)
444	  \emph{separated}. Let \(g_1, g_2 \colon W \to Z\) be morphisms. If, for every \emph{closed} point
445	  \(x \in W\), the two morphisms agree after restriction along the residue-field point
446	  \(\Spec \kappa(x) \to W\) (in Lean, $W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi g_1 =
447	  W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi g_2\(), then \)g_1 = g_2$.
448	\end{lemma}
449	\begin{proof}
450	\leanok
451	  This is the ``dense closed points \(\Rightarrow\) hom-extensionality'' connective that Mathlib does
452	  not package directly: it supplies only the single-dominant-morphism extensionality
453	  \(\mathtt{ext\_of\_isDominant}\) (a dominant morphism into a separated target is an epimorphism for
454	  the purpose of right-cancellation). We assemble the per-closed-point hypotheses into one such
455	  dominant probe.
456	  Form the coproduct \(P := \coprod_{x \in \mathrm{closedPoints}\,W} \Spec \kappa(x)\) and the morphism
457	  \(\mathrm{probe} \colon P \to W\) defined by \(\mathtt{Sigma.desc}\) of the residue-field points
458	  \(W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x\). Its topological range contains every closed point of \(W\):
459	  the inclusion \(\iota_x \colon \Spec \kappa(x) \to P\) composes with \(\mathrm{probe}\) to
460	  \(W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x\) (the defining property of \(\mathtt{Sigma.desc}\)), and the
461	  range of \(W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x\) is the singleton \(\{x\}\)
462	  (\(\mathtt{Scheme.range\_fromSpecResidueField}\)). Because \(W\) is a Jacobson space, the closed points
463	  are dense (\(\mathtt{closure\_closedPoints}\), i.e.\ \(\overline{\mathrm{closedPoints}\,W} = W\)), so
464	  the range of \(\mathrm{probe}\) is dense and \(\mathrm{probe}\) is \emph{dominant}.
465	  Componentwise, \(g_1\) and \(g_2\) agree after \(\mathrm{probe}\): precomposing with each coproduct
466	  inclusion \(\iota_x\) reduces (by \(\mathtt{Sigma.\iota\_desc}\)) to
467	  \(W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi g_1 = W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi g_2\),
468	  which is the hypothesis at the closed point \(x\). By the universal property of the coproduct
469	  (\(\mathtt{Sigma.hom\_ext}\)) this gives \(\mathrm{probe} \fatsemi g_1 = \mathrm{probe} \fatsemi g_2\).
470	  Since \(\mathrm{probe}\) is dominant and \(Z\) is separated, \(\mathtt{ext\_of\_isDominant}\) cancels it
471	  on the left to yield \(g_1 = g_2\).
472	\end{proof}

474	\leanok
475	[Global-sections constancy: two \(\bar k\)-points of a proper integral source into an affine agree]
476	  \label{lem:eq_comp_of_isAffine_of_properIntegral}
477	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.eq_comp_of_isAffine_of_properIntegral}
478	  % Project-bespoke assembly of Mathlib lemmas: NO external source. Realises, cohomology-free, the
479	  % classical "a global regular function on a proper integral variety over an algebraically closed
480	  % field is constant" --- precisely the affine-image step of Mumford's Rigidity Lemma proof quoted
481	  % above (the "complete slice into an affine maps to a single point" sentence, Ch.~II \S4, p.~43).
482	  Let \(\bar k\) be algebraically closed and let \(W\) be an integral scheme equipped with a structure
483	  morphism \(w \colon W \to \Spec \bar k\) that is universally closed (proper) and locally of finite
484	  type. Let \(g \colon W \to V\) be a morphism into an \emph{affine} scheme \(V\). Then any two
485	  \(\bar k\)-points of \(W\) --- i.e.\ any two sections \(a, b \colon \Spec \bar k \to W\) of \(w\) (so
486	  \(a \fatsemi w = \mathbf 1\) and \(b \fatsemi w = \mathbf 1\)) --- satisfy \(a \fatsemi g = b \fatsemi g\).
487	  \medskip
488	  \noindent\textbf{Every hypothesis is load-bearing} (confirmed by the iter-161 audit).
489	  \([\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]\) collapses the finite extension \(\Gamma(W)/\bar k\); integrality of
490	  \(W\) makes \(\Gamma(W)\) a domain (without it a two-point disjoint union is a counterexample);
491	  \(\mathtt{UniversallyClosed}\) is what upgrades \(\Gamma(W)\) to a \emph{field} (without it
492	  \(\mathbb A^1\), with \(\Gamma = \bar k[t]\) and two distinct \(\bar k\)-points, is a counterexample);
493	  \(\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\) gives finiteness of \(\Gamma(W)\) over \(\bar k\); and
494	  \([\mathtt{IsAffine}\ V]\) is what lets \(\mathtt{ext\_of\_isAffine}\) pin the morphism by its ring map
495	  on global sections.
496	\end{lemma}
497	\begin{proof}
498	\leanok
499	  Because \(W\) is integral and \(w\) is universally closed, the global sections
500	  \(\Gamma(W, \mathcal O_W)\) form a field (\(\mathtt{isField\_of\_universallyClosed}\)), and the structure
501	  ring map \(w^\sharp \colon \bar k \to \Gamma(W)\) (the map \(\mathtt{wk.appTop}\) after the canonical
502	  identification \(\Gamma(\Spec \bar k) \cong \bar k\)) is integral / module-finite
503	  (\(\mathtt{finite\_appTop\_of\_universallyClosed}\)). Since \(\bar k\) is algebraically closed and
504	  \(\Gamma(W)\) is a domain finite over it, that map is bijective
505	  (\(\mathtt{IsAlgClosed.ringHom\_bijective\_of\_isIntegral}\)); thus \(\Gamma(W) = \bar k\) and
506	  \(w^\sharp\) is an isomorphism on global sections --- in particular an epimorphism. Both sections
507	  \(a\), \(b\) left-invert \(w^\sharp\) on global sections (they are sections of \(w\)), so they induce the
508	  \emph{same} ring map \(a^\sharp = b^\sharp\) on \(\Gamma(W)\) (cancel the epimorphism \(w^\sharp\)). A
509	  morphism into the \emph{affine} \(V\) is pinned by its global-sections ring map
510	  (\(\mathtt{ext\_of\_isAffine}\)), whence \(a \fatsemi g = b \fatsemi g\). This realises, without
511	  cohomology, the classical ``proper connected variety into an affine is a single point'' step
512	  quoted from Mumford above.
513	\end{proof}

515	\leanok
516	[Integrality descends to a retract of an integral scheme]
517	  \label{lem:isIntegral_of_retract_of_integral}
518	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.isIntegral_of_retract}
519	  % NOTE: (iter-162 review) project-bespoke (elementary topology + algebra); NO external source.
520	  % LANDED axiom-clean as `AlgebraicGeometry.isIntegral_of_retract`, stated generically for any
521	  % retract `r : S ⟶ T`, `pr : T ⟶ S` with `r ≫ pr = 𝟙 S` of an integral `T` (more general than
522	  % the `X`/`X×Y`/`p_1` instance below). PROOF DIVERGENCE (cosmetic, sound either way): the Lean
523	  % proves reducedness PER-STALK (each `S.stalk x` embeds via `pr.stalkMap` into the reduced
524	  % `T.stalk`, then `isReduced_of_isReduced_stalk`), not via the global-sections split-injection
525	  % the prose below describes. Plan/blueprint-writer may align the prose to the stalk route.
526	  Let \(X \times Y\) be an integral scheme (here it is geometrically irreducible and reduced over
527	  \(\bar k\)). Suppose \(X\) is a \emph{retract} of \(X \times Y\): there is a section
528	  \(s \colon X \to X \times Y\) of the first projection \(p_1\), i.e.\ \(s \fatsemi p_1 = \mathbf 1_X\).
529	  Then \(X\) is integral.
530	\end{lemma}
531	\begin{proof}
532	\leanok
533	  Integrality is reducedness together with irreducibility, and the retract supplies each.
534	  \emph{Reduced.} The section identity \(s \fatsemi p_1 = \mathbf 1_X\) makes the pullback on global
535	  sections \(p_1^\sharp \colon \mathcal O_X(X) \to \mathcal O_{X \times Y}(X \times Y)\) split
536	  injective (it is left-inverted by \(s^\sharp\)). A split-injective ring map into the reduced ring
537	  \(\mathcal O_{X \times Y}(X \times Y)\) embeds \(\mathcal O_X(X)\) into a reduced ring, so it too is
538	  reduced (\(\mathtt{isReduced\_of\_injective}\) on the split injection); hence \(X\) is reduced.
539	  \emph{Irreducible.} The first projection \(p_1 \colon X \times Y \to X\) is surjective because it
540	  admits the section \(s\) (every point of \(X\) is \(p_1(s(\cdot))\)). The continuous image of an
541	  irreducible space under a surjection is irreducible; as \(X \times Y\) is irreducible and \(p_1\) is a
542	  continuous surjection onto \(X\), the space of \(X\) is irreducible.
543	  Reduced and irreducible together give that \(X\) is integral. This is elementary --- no cohomology,
544	  no external citation --- and feeds the Step-1 geometric assembly below, where the proper slice
545	  \(X_y \cong X\) must be presented as proper \emph{integral}.
546	\end{proof}

593	\section{The Milne \S I.3 chain: additivity, homomorphisms, extension}
594	This section assembles, from the proven Rigidity Lemma, the genus-\(0\) base case. The two
595	load-bearing ingredients of the \textbf{primary route} are the additive decomposition over a
596	product (\cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}, Milne Corollary~1.5) and the concrete $\mathbb
597	G_m\(-scaling action \)\sigma_\times\( on \)\mathbb P^1$ (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1}); from these
598	the base case ``every morphism \(\mathbb P^1 \to A\) is constant'' falls out \emph{in one shot}
599	(\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}): the scaling fixed point \(0\) collapses one axis of the
600	decomposition, giving \(f(\lambda x) = f(x)\) and hence constancy on the dense \(\mathbb G_m\). The
601	classical \(\mathbb G_a\)-additive homomorphism step
602	(\cref{lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial}) and the triviality lemma \(\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A) = 0\)
603	(\cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}) are retained as the Milne-faithful alternative but are
604	\emph{not} on the genus-\(0\) critical path. None of this uses the theorem of the cube, and --- crucially
605	--- none of it uses Milne's Theorem~3.2 / Lemma~3.3 (the rational-map extension): because $f
606	\colon \mathbb P^1 \to A\( is \emph{already} a total morphism out of the complete \)\mathbb
607	P^1$, there is no defect map to extend over a complete surface (see
608	\cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}). Corollary~1.2 (\cref{lem:av_regular_map_is_hom}) and
609	Theorem~3.2 (\cref{lem:rational_map_to_av_extends}) are retained in this chapter only as
610	Route-A inputs (the Albanese universal property) and are explicitly \emph{off} the genus-\(0\)
611	path. The genus-\(0\) output is exactly the content of Milne's Proposition~3.9, ``every rational
612	(here: regular) map \(\mathbb P^1 \to A\) is constant''.

622	  Theorem~1.1 --- our \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma} --- via Corollary~1.5 (additivity,
623	  \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}) composed with the total \(\mathbb G_m\)-scaling
624	  action \(\sigma_\times\) (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1}): the scaling fixed point \(0\) collapses one
625	  axis of the decomposition, yielding \(f(\lambda x) = f(x)\) and hence constancy on the dense
626	  \(\mathbb G_m\) (the primary \(\mathbb G_m\)-scaling shortcut; see
627	  \cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}). This route does not even use the triviality of
628	  \(\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A)\) (\cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}), which is retained only for the
629	  Milne-faithful \(\mathbb G_a\)-additive alternative. Neither the theorem of the cube nor Milne's Theorem~3.2 /
630	  Lemma~3.3 (rational-map extension) is used: since \(f \colon \mathbb P^1 \to A\) is already a

634	  projectivity, the seesaw/square principle, dual abelian varieties, polarizations, and the
635	  Poincar\'e sheaf --- none on the genus-\(0\) path. For Route~A: the Albanese universal property
636	  that the positive-genus arm needs (Milne Proposition~6.1/6.4, \S III.6) is \emph{also} cube-free,
637	  resting on Theorem~3.2 (\cref{lem:rational_map_to_av_extends}) + Corollary~1.2/1.5 --- which is

642	[Additive decomposition over a product (Milne Corollary 1.5)]
643	  \label{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}

661	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Corollary 1.5, \S I.1, doc p.~9 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 15)
662	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "COROLLARY 1.5. Let V and W be complete varieties over k with k-rational
663	  % points v0 and w0, and let p and q be the projection maps V×W→V and V×W→W. Let A be an
664	  % abelian variety. Then a morphism h:V×W→A such that h(v0,w0)=0 can be written uniquely as
665	  % h = f∘p + g∘q with f:V→A and g:W→A morphisms such that f(v0)=0 and g(w0)=0."
666	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Corollary~1.5.}
667	  Let \(V\), \(W\) be complete varieties over \(\bar k\) with \(\bar k\)-points \(v_0 \in V\),
668	  \(w_0 \in W\), and let \(p \colon V \times W \to V\), \(q \colon V \times W \to W\) be the
669	  projections. Let \(A\) be an abelian variety over \(\bar k\). Then a morphism
670	  \(h \colon V \times W \to A\) with \(h(v_0, w_0) = 0\) can be written uniquely as
671	  \(h = f \circ p + g \circ q\), with morphisms \(f \colon V \to A\), \(g \colon W \to A\) satisfying
672	  \(f(v_0) = 0\) and \(g(w_0) = 0\).
673	  \emph{Lean encoding.} In the project \(A\) is a group object \texttt{[GrpObj A]} (not assumed
674	  commutative). The ``\(+\)'' here is the binary operation that \texttt{GrpObj} induces on each
675	  hom-set \(\mathrm{Hom}(X, A)\): for \(u, v \colon X \to A\), \(u + v\) is the composite
676	  \(X \xrightarrow{\langle u, v\rangle} A \times A \xrightarrow{\;\mathrm{mul}\;} A\) (and
677	  \(f \circ p + g \circ q\) is built this way from the projections). The decomposition is therefore
678	  read \emph{multiplicatively} as \(h = (f \circ p) \cdot (g \circ q)\) in the group
679	  \(\mathrm{Hom}(V \times W, A)\), and does \emph{not} require \(A\) commutative; commutativity of
680	  abelian varieties (Milne Cor 1.4, a separate consequence of \cref{lem:av_regular_map_is_hom})
681	  is needed only to rewrite the product symmetrically/additively, not for the statement or its
682	  proof. The prover should infer the hom-set group operation from the \texttt{GrpObj} instance,
683	  not from a \texttt{CommGrpObj} instance.
684	\end{lemma}
685	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. Set f=h|V×{w0}, g=h|{v0}×W, and identify V×{w0} and {v0}×W with
686	% V and W. On points, f(v) = h(v,w0) and g(w) = h(v0,w), and so φ =df h − (f∘p + g∘q) is the
687	% map that sends (v,w) ↦ h(v,w) − h(v,w0) − h(v0,w). Thus φ(V×{w0}) = 0 = φ({v0}×W) and so the
688	% theorem shows that φ = 0." [Milne, Cor 1.5, \S I.1, doc p.~9--10.]
689	\begin{proof}
690	  \leanok
691	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Corollary~1.5.}
692	  Define \(f := h|_{V \times \{w_0\}}\) and \(g := h|_{\{v_0\} \times W}\), identifying
693	  \(V \times \{w_0\} \cong V\) and \(\{v_0\} \times W \cong W\); on points \(f(v) = h(v, w_0)\) and
694	  \(g(w) = h(v_0, w)\). Form the difference
695	  \[ \varphi := h - (f \circ p + g \circ q) \colon V \times W \to A, \qquad
696	     \varphi(v, w) = h(v, w) - h(v, w_0) - h(v_0, w) \]
697	  (the subtraction is in the group \(A\), i.e.\ \(\varphi\) is the composite of
698	  \((h, -(f \circ p + g \circ q))\) with the group law of \(A\)). Then \(\varphi\) vanishes on both
699	  axes: \(\varphi(V \times \{w_0\}) = 0 = \varphi(\{v_0\} \times W)\) (direct computation, using
700	  \(h(v_0, w_0) = 0\)). In particular \(\varphi\) collapses the complete axis \(V \times \{w_0\}\) to
701	  the single point \(0 \in A\), so by the Rigidity Lemma (\cref{thm:rigidity_lemma}, with \(V\)
702	  complete and \(V \times W\) geometrically irreducible) \(\varphi\) factors through the projection
703	  \(q\); but it also vanishes on \(\{v_0\} \times W\), which is a section of \(q\), so the factoring
704	  morphism is identically \(0\), i.e.\ \(\varphi \equiv 0\). Hence \(h = f \circ p + g \circ q\).
705	  Uniqueness: restricting any such decomposition to the axes recovers \(f\) and \(g\) as the
706	  axis-restrictions of \(h\).
707	\end{proof}

731	  % of a homomorphism with a translation."
732	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Corollary~1.2.}
733	  Every regular map \(\alpha \colon A \to B\) of abelian varieties over \(\bar k\) is the composite
734	  of a homomorphism with a translation; equivalently, if \(\alpha(0_A) = 0_B\) then \(\alpha\) is a
735	  homomorphism, i.e.\ \(\alpha(a + a') = \alpha(a) + \alpha(a')\) for all \(a, a' \in A\).
736	\end{lemma}
737	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. The regular map α will send the k-rational point 0 of A to a
738	% k-rational point b of B. After composing α with translation by −b, we may assume that
739	% α(0)=0. Consider the map φ:A×A→B, φ(a,a′)=α(a+a′) − α(a) − α(a′). By this we mean that φ is
740	% the difference of the two regular maps A×A →(m)→ A →(α)→ B and A×A →(α×α)→ B×B →(m)→ B, which
741	% is a regular map. Then φ(A×0)=0=φ(0×A) and so φ=0. This means that α is a homomorphism."
742	% [Milne, Cor 1.2, \S I.1, doc p.~9.]
743	\begin{proof}
744	  \leanok
745	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Corollary~1.2.}
746	  The image \(\alpha(0_A)\) is a \(\bar k\)-point \(b\) of \(B\); composing \(\alpha\) with translation by
747	  \(-b\) we may assume \(\alpha(0_A) = 0_B\), and it suffices to show such an \(\alpha\) is a
748	  homomorphism. Form
749	  \[ \varphi \colon A \times A \to B, \qquad \varphi(a, a') = \alpha(a + a') - \alpha(a) - \alpha(a'), \]
750	  the difference of the two regular maps \(A \times A \xrightarrow{m} A \xrightarrow{\alpha} B\)
751	  and \(A \times A \xrightarrow{\alpha \times \alpha} B \times B \xrightarrow{m} B\). Then
752	  \(\varphi(0_A, 0_A) = 0\) and \(\varphi\) vanishes on both axes,
753	  \(\varphi(A \times 0) = 0 = \varphi(0 \times A)\) (since \(\alpha(0) = 0\)). By the additive
754	  decomposition \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} (with \(V = W = A\)), the axis-restrictions
755	  of \(\varphi\) being \(0\) force \(\varphi \equiv 0\); equivalently, \(\varphi\) collapses the complete
756	  axis \(A \times 0\) and vanishes on \(0 \times A\), so Rigidity gives \(\varphi = 0\). Hence
757	  \(\alpha(a + a') = \alpha(a) + \alpha(a')\), i.e.\ \(\alpha\) is a homomorphism.
758	\end{proof}

777	  path; the 4th route (\cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}, \cref{def:gaTranslationP1}) closes the

785	% doc p.~17.]
